assumptions: The question is assessed in the context of consolidated IFRS financial statements.
The declared intragroup dividend has created a recognised intragroup receivable/payable that is monetary in nature.
The hedge being considered targets foreign currency risk only, not the full economics of the dividend.
At least one of the group entities involved has a different functional currency, creating potential FX gains or losses on consolidation.

approaches.label: General prohibition on intragroup hedged items
approaches.reasoning: In consolidated financial statements, hedged items must generally relate to external parties. Intragroup assets, liabilities and transactions are normally not eligible hedged items.
approaches.practical_implication: A hedge over an intragroup dividend exposure is not supportable unless a specific IFRS 9 exception applies.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.5 limits hedged items in consolidation to exposures with parties external to the reporting entity.

approaches.label: Fair value hedge of FX risk on intragroup monetary item
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 allows the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item to qualify in consolidated financial statements when FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated. The FX component can be designated if it is separately identifiable and reliably measurable.
conditions: the dividend creates a recognised intragroup monetary receivable/payable
the designated risk is foreign currency risk
the FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation because the entities have different functional currencies
the FX component is separately identifiable and reliably measurable
formal designation, documentation and hedge effectiveness requirements are met
approaches.practical_implication: If these conditions are met, the group can document a hedge of the FX component of the intragroup dividend receivable.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 permits foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item in consolidated financial statements when consolidation does not fully eliminate the FX exposure.
Paragraphs 6.3.7 and B6.3.8 allow designation of a risk component only if it is separately identifiable and reliably measurable.
Paragraphs 6.4.1 and 6.5.2(a) require formal documentation, effectiveness assessment and support fair value hedge designation for a recognised asset or liability.

approaches.label: Cash flow hedge of forecast intragroup transaction
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 has a separate exception for highly probable forecast intragroup transactions whose FX risk affects consolidated profit or loss. That route does not address an already recognised intragroup receivable.
approaches.practical_implication: This is not the relevant hedge-accounting basis once the dividend has become a recognised receivable.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 and B6.3.5 allow certain forecast intragroup FX exposures only when they are highly probable and affect consolidated profit or loss.
Paragraph 6.5.2(b) defines cash flow hedges for highly probable forecast transactions or variability in cash flows.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge
approaches.reasoning: IFRIC 16 addresses hedges of foreign currency risk arising from a net investment in a foreign operation. The hedged item is an amount of net assets, not an intragroup dividend receivable.
approaches.practical_implication: Net investment hedge guidance cannot be used to justify hedging the dividend receivable itself.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraphs 10–12 restrict the hedged risk to FX differences on a net investment and the hedged item to an amount of net assets.
Paragraph 14 requires IFRS 9 designation, documentation and effectiveness requirements for a qualifying net investment hedge.
